reasoning: The base rate for an armed resistance group officially agreeing to disarm in a short timeframe without a conclusive political settlement is extremely low. While there were reports in early October 2025 of Hamas agreeing to a U.S.-brokered Phase 1 ceasefire and hostage release deal, subsequent statements by senior Hamas officials consistently denied any official commitment to disarm. By late October, Hamas officials were recorded stating that disarmament is "problematic" and postponed it to a future "national framework," asserting they intend to maintain a grip on Gaza security. Even smaller-scale local deals proposed in early November (like the safe passage of 200 fighters in Rafah) faced pushback and were denied by Israel. There is no official announcement or concrete policy directive by Hamas's widely acknowledged leadership committing to unilateral or conditional disarmament by December 31, 2025. Given the strict resolution criteria—requiring an official announcement, not merely a future contingency or informal statement—the likelihood of this occurring before Dec 31 drops precipitously as the deadline approaches.
